title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about a documentary filmmaker. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: What the Darkness Teaches
content.subtitle: A film critic turns the camera on herself
content.text:
Elena Varga had spent fifteen years writing about other people's films before she decided to make one herself. Her transition from critic to director was widely reported as either brave or foolish, depending on who you asked. [31]Those who knew her work as a critic argued that she possessed a rare analytical understanding of what made films succeed or fail — an understanding, they suggested, that was a far more reliable foundation for filmmaking than instinct alone.[/31]

Her first film, The Edge of the Frame, was a documentary about a group of elderly residents in a remote Hungarian village who had never visited a city. Varga spent three years living among her subjects, filming quietly with a small crew. [32]Critics were struck less by the film's technical accomplishment — which was considerable — than by the almost invisible relationship between camera and subject, a quality rarely achieved by directors with decades more experience.[/32]

The film's central question was not about rural life or modernisation, as some had expected, but about the nature of attention itself. [33]Varga has described her time in the village as a kind of re-education. 'In the city, we look at things in order to identify them and move on,' she wrote in the published diary accompanying the film. 'In the village, people looked at things in order to know them. That is an entirely different act.'[/33]

The commercial performance of The Edge of the Frame was modest. It screened at seventeen international festivals and won awards at four, but found only a limited theatrical release. [34]Varga has said she expected nothing different. Documentary films that resist narrative momentum and refuse to offer conclusions are, she acknowledges freely, difficult propositions for distributors.[/34] Her response was to begin work immediately on a second project.

What distinguishes Varga from many documentary filmmakers working today is her resistance to the idea that films should argue for something. [35]Where the dominant mode of contemporary documentary is advocacy — films designed to change minds or prompt action — Varga's work asks only that the viewer be present. She is not trying to convince anyone of anything.[/35]

[36]This approach has divided audiences and critics almost as sharply as it has attracted devoted followers. Those who find her films transformative describe a quality of concentrated attention that they rarely experience elsewhere; those who find them frustrating cite the absence of any clear message or direction.[/36] Varga herself seems content with this. In a recent interview, she said simply: 'A film that makes everyone comfortable has probably said nothing at all.'

questions.question: What did those who knew Elena Varga's critical work believe about her transition to directing?
options: A) They thought she lacked the emotional sensitivity needed to direct films.
B) They felt her analytical knowledge of film gave her a strong basis for directing.
C) They believed she should have started making films much earlier in her career.
D) They expected her to struggle because she had no technical filmmaking skills.
questions.explanation: The text says her critics argued she possessed a 'rare analytical understanding' that was 'a far more reliable foundation for filmmaking than instinct alone.'

questions.question: What most impressed critics about The Edge of the Frame?
options: A) The barely noticeable bond between the filmmaker and her subjects.
B) The film's innovative use of camera techniques and lighting.
C) The depth of research into rural Hungarian history and culture.
D) The clear narrative structure that guided viewers through the story.
questions.explanation: Critics were struck by 'the almost invisible relationship between camera and subject,' which is described as a quality rarely achieved even by experienced directors.

questions.question: What did Varga learn during her time in the village?
options: A) That rural communities are fundamentally different from urban ones.
B) That documentary filmmaking requires years of dedicated preparation.
C) That attention in city environments is more productive than in rural ones.
D) That there is a qualitative difference between identifying something and truly knowing it.
questions.explanation: Varga contrasts the city habit of looking at things 'in order to identify them and move on' with the village habit of looking 'in order to know them,' calling it 'an entirely different act.'

questions.question: Why did The Edge of the Frame receive only a limited theatrical release?
options: A) The film was considered too short for standard cinema distribution.
B) Festival judges were not impressed by the film's unconventional style.
C) Films without clear storylines or conclusions are hard to distribute commercially.
D) Varga refused to allow the film to be shown in mainstream cinemas.
questions.explanation: Varga acknowledges that documentary films which 'resist narrative momentum and refuse to offer conclusions are difficult propositions for distributors.'

questions.question: How does Varga's filmmaking approach differ from most documentary makers today?
options: A) She uses more advanced filming techniques than most of her contemporaries.
B) She does not seek to persuade viewers but simply invites them to be present.
C) She focuses exclusively on rural communities rather than urban subjects.
D) She produces films that are deliberately short to maintain audience attention.
questions.explanation: Unlike the 'dominant mode' of advocacy documentary, Varga's work 'asks only that the viewer be present' and 'is not trying to convince anyone of anything.'

questions.question: What does the final paragraph tell us about the reception of Varga's films?
options: A) They inspire strong feelings in viewers, ranging from deep appreciation to frustration.
B) They are universally admired by critics but fail to attract general audiences.
C) They have recently begun to attract larger audiences after years of obscurity.
D) They are popular mainly with younger audiences who appreciate experimental cinema.
questions.explanation: The paragraph describes a sharp divide: devoted followers find them 'transformative' while frustrated viewers 'cite the absence of any clear message or direction.'
